- A pyrrolopyrimidine core with an N-linked fluorophenyl amide.
- A morpholine attached via a short alkyl linker on the nitrogen of the amide.
- An additional fluoro substituent on the phenyl ring.
- The molecule would be:

"COC1=CC2=C(NC(=O)C3=CN=C(N4CCOCC4)C=C3C(F)=C2C=C1F)C(F)=CC=C"

But this is not a complete SMILES, I need to check closure and structure.

Alternatively, let's propose a somewhat simpler SMILES with these ideas:

"C1=CN=C2C=CN=C(N2C3=CC(F)=C(C=C3)C(=O)NC4CCOCC4)N1"

This represents a fused purine-like system (C1=CN=C2C=CN=C(N2...)N1) with an amide linking a fluorophenyl ring (C3=CC(F)=C(C=C3)) attached to a morpholine ring (NC4CCOCC4).

This is unique compared to the two given molecules.

Note: Replaced the piperazine ring with morpholine (NCCOCCN to NCCOCCO) by substituting a nitrogen in the piperazine to oxygen in morpholine; the morpholine SMILES is N1CCOCC1.

Better to encode morpholine 6-member ring with one oxygen and five atoms: O1CCNCC1

The morpholine ring: N1CCOCC1

Therefore, retain the same connection point, but use morpholine instead of piperazine:

So side chain becomes OCCCN3CCOCC3

SMILES: CC1=NN=C2C=C(CC3=C(F)C=CC=C3F)NN21C(=O)NO

But this seems too short and possibly not fused properly.

Alternatively, design a scaffold with the pyrazolopyridine core fused as in input compounds and attach on the side a hydroxamic acid substituent:

Bringing in the fused heterocycle with fluorophenyl, plus a linker to hydroxamic acid.
